title: Information Search Within a Web Page: Modeling the Full Sequence of Eye Movement Decisions, Subjective Value Updating, and First Clicks
abstract: Online retail settings often present shoppers with large, complex choice sets where they need to quickly and dynamically weigh the benefits and costs of search within each web page. We build a model of information search within a web page using eye-tracking data collected during two incentive-compatible online shopping experiments, in which participants browsed the websites of two different clothing retailers (Experiments 1 and 2), as well as previously reported data from a laboratory experiment involving choices among snack food assortments (Experiment 3). Our model incorporates features that build upon recent advances in descriptive and normative models of information sampling and search in psychology and economics. First, our model captures how people decide where to look by treating eye fixations on clickable options as a series of “split-second” decisions that depend on estimates of option attractiveness and navigation effort. Second, our model assumes that the value of each option is learned via Bayesian updating. Third, the choice to end search on the web page depends on a dynamic decision threshold. Our model outperforms benchmarks that assume random search, instant learning, fixed thresholds, nonheterogeneous thresholds, and stochastic accumulator stopping rules. Explicitly modeling the sequence of eye fixation decisions results in accurate counterfactual simulations of the effects of hypothetical product orderings on search duration and quality as verified using experimental manipulation, and it can be applied flexibly to a wide range of web-page layouts. Systematic differences across experiments highlight the importance of accounting for product familiarity, choice-set size, and the role of category outside options.

article.1. Introduction: Internet shopping often involves consumers browsing a set of optionswithina single web page leading up to a click decision, akin to choosing a product from a shelf display in a brick-and-mortar store. Understanding how shoppers visually navigate a large set of options on a screen is important for online retailers when designing web-page layouts and predicting information search and purchase decisions. In this paper, we propose a model that treats information search as a series of discrete “split-second” decisions to visually examine different click options on a web page. Depending on the web-page design, visual search may eventually lead to a click that takes the customer to a new page containing more information (e.g., about a specific product or product category) or adds the product to the customer’s shopping cart. We collected eye-tracking data during two incentive-compatible online shopping experiments in which participants browsed the websites of two different clothing retailers (Experiments 1 and 2). We focus on modeling information search on a common “landing page,” which contained rows of links to featured products and general product categories. Additionally, we apply our model to previously reported eye-tracking data from a laboratory experiment (Reutskaja et al. 2011) involving a more “clean” stylized setting where participants chose among assortments of snack foods across multiple trials (Experiment 3).Figure 1shows the displays used in these three experiments. Eye tracking provides a precise account of the information sampled on a single page, which is not available in traditional clickstream data. Eye tracking also enables more naturalistic and unobtrusive recording compared with other process tracing methods, like MouseLab, which requires individuals to mouse over options to reveal them and may alter the search process (Lohse and Johnson 1996). The recorded stream of eye movements consists offixations, defined as when the eyes are focused on a single location for at least 50 milliseconds, andsaccades, defined as when the eyes are rapidly moving from one location to another (Holmqvist et al. 2011). We use fixations as a measure of visual search because only fixated information can be processed in detail. To analyze shoppers’ fixation sequences, we divide up each web-page or choice-set display intoareas of interest(AOIs), which also contain click options in the three experiments. Thus, each fixation corresponds to a split-second AOI choice that provides the viewer with a small sample of visual information (e.g., five to seven letters when reading text or one product image) (seeRayner 1998for a seminal review). As shown inFigure 1, in Experiments 1 and 2, the rows of products were partitioned into multiple “product AOIs,” each with a single product and a link to a more detailed page for that product. The product AOIs contained several types of information (e.g., product style, color, price, etc.), so several fixations might be required to learn all the information. We often observed shoppers fixating multiple times on the same AOI either through consecutive fixations or through nonconsecutive revisits. Additionally, we delineated one “category AOI” that contained all the links to general categories of products and represented an outside option for shoppers who either did not like any of the product AOIs or preferred to navigate to a specific category more efficiently. In Experiment 3, the AOIs were defined by dividing up the screen into 2×2, 3×3, or 4×4 grids of product AOIs, depending on the assortment size. Using these data, we build a two-stage search model where each eye fixation event consists of a stage 1 decision about where to look for information and a stage 2 decision about whether to click on the currently fixated AOI and leave the page or continue searching. First, our model captures how people decide where to look by treating the sequence of eye fixations on different AOIs or click options as a series of split-second decisions. Importantly, this builds upon existing eye-tracking studies that test different stopping rules and how visual attention predicts choice within large assortments (Reutskaja et al. 2011,Thomas et al. 2021). However, this prior research does not model the actual sequence of eye fixations as decisions. Second, we assume that shoppers learn information about options through eye fixations via Bayesian updating, which allows us to capture differences in the learning rate of product preferences across shoppers, as well as examine how the learning rate is related to other search characteristics. Third, we define a decision threshold that determines when search ends that may change as search progresses. Across all three experiments, we demonstrate that (1) where people look depends on AOI attractiveness and navigation effort; (2) subjective value updating is more akin to a Bayesian updating process rather than instant learning; and (3) the decision threshold that determines when search ends decreases over time, which reflects a long-term explore-exploit heuristic (March 1991,Dellaert and Häubl 2012,Huang and Hutchinson 2013). Moreover, we demonstrate that our model outperforms various benchmark models with different components removed and alternative stopping rule specifications, and it is applicable to both settings where one or multiple search sequences are observed per individual. Finally, we test how well our model accounts for differences in search behavior based on exogenous variation of shopping goals and product layout. In Experiment 1, shoppers were randomly assigned to utilitarian versus hedonic shopping goal conditions (Holbrook and Hirschman 1982), and we test whether our model captures differences in search length. Additionally, it is important for managers to consider the strategic goals of web-page design, including facilitating product search and increasing click-through rates and/or product purchase. Specifically, the arrangement or layout of products may influence how long shoppers spend on the page and what products they find. Across the three experiments, we use both counterfactual simulation and experimental manipulation to investigate how different preference-based product layouts affect search length and clicks. Our model demonstrates the importance of accounting for product preferences and fixation effort during the visual search process, with our results revealing that search and information acquisition are quite complex, even within a single web page. Firms can utilize our modeling approach to simulate predictions of how consumers may search different web-page layouts, and in particular, gain insights into the trade-offs between search duration and quality. Systematic differences across experiments also suggest that consumer familiarity with the products and choice-set size are important factors in the search process, including the likelihood of selecting an outside option, such as the category AOI; the speed at which preference is learned; and the overall search length. In the rest of the paper, we review mathematical models of information processing and search originally developed in cognitive psychology and how they relate to search models developed in economics. We specify a model of eye fixation and click decisions, which captures how people decide where to look, how they learn or update their preferences based on the information obtained from visual search, and how they decide to end search and select an option. We present our estimation procedure and the posterior predictive checks that measure how well our model captures shopper search sequences. We then review the design and findings of the three experiments.
article.2. Literature Review: Models of Informat: Multioption displays are commonly used to studysequential samplingmodels in psychology. These models are typically descriptive and capture observed behavioral processes involved in information accumulation during choice among a relatively small set of options (usually two to three). They assume that evidence stochastically accumulates over time for different options until a boundary is reached, at which point the decision maker selects an option. Random walk models (Busemeyer and Townsend 1993), drift diffusion models (Krajbich et al. 2010,Satomura et al. 2014), and Poisson race models (Huang and Hutchinson 2008,Otter et al. 2008b) all fall under the umbrella of sequential sampling models. Sequential sampling models have been shown to be psychologically and neurologically plausible accounts of fast decisions (split second or within a few seconds), and they can explain differences in reaction times for simple versus complex choices, speed-accuracy trade-offs, memory retrieval, and serial position effects (Townsend and Ashby 1983,Smith and Ratcliff 2004,Otter et al. 2008b). Moreover, examining multiple options before choice resemblessequential searchmodels in economics. Such models are normative and prescribe the consumer’s optimal forward-looking search strategy. The goal is often to derive the optimal solution for problems spanning relatively long time horizons (e.g., technology investment, job search, etc.). These models typically assume rational behavior via a fully forward-looking dynamic process, and thus, they often include restrictive simplifying properties, such as full or partial prior knowledge about the distribution from which observed values are drawn or full information from a single sample (e.g.,Weitzman 1979,Lippman and McCardle 1991). These models have been applied within the marketing literature to consumer and firm search problems (Kim et al. 2010,Honka and Chintagunta 2016,Chen and Yao 2017). Similar to the sequential sampling models in psychology, the solution of a sequential search model often consists of a decision boundary or “reservation utility” that defines the stopping rule. A notable difference between the two sets of models is that the primary goal of sequential sampling models in psychology is to accurately describe the decision process underlying fast, short-term choices (i.e., descriptive models), whereas the primary goal of sequential search models in economics is to find the optimal solution for relatively long-term choices (i.e., normative models). Recent advances and extensions to both sets of models have paralleled the increased availability of more detailed data collection methods, including clickstream and eye tracking. For example, sequential sampling models in psychology have been extended to capture how the visual display of options may impact search (e.g.,Towal et al. 2013,Thomas et al. 2021). In economics, sequential search models have been extended primarily to allow for more generalized settings (e.g., revisits, incremental learning, etc.) while still maintaining the assumption of optimality (e.g.,Lippman and McCardle 1991,Chick and Frazier 2012,Koulayev 2014,Ursu et al. 2020). For our model of information search within a web page, we combine elements of sequential sampling models from psychology and sequential search models from economics. In the following subsections, we highlight three areas where sequential sampling models and sequential search models originally diverged. We discuss how richer data have facilitated new advances, which have led to some degree of convergence among decision models in psychology and economics. Also, we discuss how we incorporate these advances into our model of visual search within a web page.Table 1provides an overview of the characteristics of both sets of models, recent advances, and our model. Specifically, we review (1)display navigationor how people determine where to look, (2)value updatingor how people incorporate information from visual search into the subjective value of options, and (3)decision thresholdsor how people decide when to stop searching and select a final option. Table 1.Comparison of Current Sequential Sampling Models in Psychology and Sequential Search Models in Economics to Our Model of Fixation Decisions Table 1.Comparison of Current Sequential Sampling Models in Psychology and Sequential Search Models in Economics to Our Model of Fixation Decisions 2.1. Display Navigation Traditionally in sequential sampling models, information accumulation is modeled as a passive process rather than an explicit consumer decision (Ratcliff and Smith 2004). Eye-tracking data can supplement these models as a direct measure of evidence accumulation. Related work has treated fixations as dependent on the value, position, and visual salience of options (Van der Lans et al. 2008,Chandon et al. 2009,Towal et al. 2013) and as following specific patterns within latent states (Liechty et al. 2003,Wedel et al. 2008,Stüttgen et al. 2012,Shi et al. 2013). Importantly, this work demonstrates that properties of the visual display may impact information search and choice. People may even learn to “look for high value” with repeated exposures to displays with preferred options in specific locations (Colas and Lu 2017). Two recent papers have derived numerical approximations and closed-form solutions for the optimal sampling process of visual fixations by assuming that individuals are updating their beliefs in a Bayesian fashion and selecting the optimal options to fixate on to resolve uncertainty; however, these models are computationally intensive, and they are limited to simple choice among two or three options (Callaway et al. 2021,Jang et al. 2021). Sequential search models in economics tend to involve an optimal stopping rule that describes the number of items to search (e.g.,McCall 1970,Weitzman 1979). The search sequence is often modeled as a set of explicit utility-maximizing decisions (i.e., based on reservation values) but without regard to the arrangement of options (e.g., the secretary problem). With the increased availability of clickstream data, firms can track customers across different web pages, which allows researchers to observe and model intermediate decisions, such as product sorting and examination of deeper product information (Chen and Yao 2017,De los Santos and Koulayev 2017). We incorporate both perspectives by treating visual search as part of the decision-making process and explicitly modeling eye fixations on the landing page as a series of split-second decisions. We benchmark our model against models that assume search is relatively “random” and does not depend on AOI attractiveness or visual location to demonstrate how these factors influence eye fixation decisions. Incorporating how people navigate the web page or computer screen also allows us to make predictions of how people would search if the layout was different. 2.2. Value Updating Sequential sampling models in psychology assume that information about different options is accumulated incrementally (and usually stochastically) before a decision is made. For example, the drift diffusion model for binary choice assumes that the drift rate toward the boundary for one option depends on the individual’s relative preference for that option (versus the alternative). Recent work relates evidence accumulation to visual attention. For example, theattentionaldrift diffusion model allows drift to depend on visual fixations (e.g.,Krajbich et al. 2010,Fisher 2017).Thomas et al. (2021)demonstrate that models that allow the subjective value of options to depend on the amount of visual attention improve fit to data from multialternative choice experiments. In contrast, classic sequential search models in economics often assume that full information is acquired after a single sample (i.e., no incremental learning). For example, theWeitzman (1979)model of sequential search with recall allows for sampling across multiple options but only a single sample of each option because the true values of the options are learned instantly. Generalized optimal search solutions allow for multiple searches with learning via Bayesian updating to resolve option uncertainty (Lippman and McCardle 1991). A growing body of research has incorporated the incremental accumulation of product knowledge into the model framework that accounts for the unlimited information available within e-commerce settings (Branco et al. 2012) as well as nonconsecutive revisits to options (Chick and Frazier 2012,Ursu et al. 2020). In our model, we allow shoppers to learn incrementally about the AOI options through Bayesian updating (e.g.,Erdem and Keane 1996,Ernst and Banks 2002,Stocker and Simoncelli 2006). Bayesian updating allows us to examine how fast individuals learn product information via eye fixations. We benchmark our model against an alternative in which the subjective value of options is learned “instantly” (i.e., with a single fixation). 2.3. Decision Thresholds In both sequential sampling and sequential search models, there is typically a decision boundary that determines when search ends. In sequential sampling models, this boundary is interpreted as representing how much information or evidence the individual needs to make a decision. In sequential search models, it represents the utility of the option in hand that makes the individual indifferent between continuing and stopping search (i.e., reservation utility) (Weitzman 1979). Recent eye-tracking work has compared optimal stopping rules with satisficing rules (Simon 1955), where search ends when the consumer finds an option that is “good enough.”Reutskaja et al. (2011)demonstrate that the probability of ending search increases with each passing eye fixation. Their findings provide evidence against an optimal stopping rule with zero search costs, which predicts that people will examine as many items as possible within the time limit of an initial search phase and then select the best item. Models that allow for satisficing behavior are useful for modeling choice among large sets of options (Stüttgen et al. 2012).Thomas et al. (2021)compare satisficing and stochastic accumulator stopping rules for large choice sets ranging from 9 to 36 options. Similar frameworks have been used to model the incremental accumulation of product knowledge with purchase and no-purchase thresholds (Branco et al. 2012) and model how shoppers visit different areas of a physical store and decide whether to continue shopping or check out after each visit (Hui et al. 2009). Moreover, decision thresholds have been broadly used in areas of perceptual and cognitive psychology, including signal detection theory (Luan et al. 2011) and decision rule learning (Busemeyer and Myung 1992). Both sequential sampling and sequential search models traditionally assume that the threshold is fixed; however, work in both areas has proposed that thresholds may change across the course of the decision-making process. For example, drift diffusion models capture binary choice with two boundaries, and there is empirical evidence both for and against collapsing boundaries, often interpreted as urgency signals (Ditterich 2006,Hawkins et al. 2015).Tajima et al. (2016)andFudenberg et al. (2018)demonstrate that under certain conditions, collapsing boundaries may represent an optimal strategy. Laboratory participants in stylized search tasks exhibit declining thresholds (Adam 2001,Brown et al. 2011,Koulayev 2014), which may reflect rising search costs or declining expectations. Declining thresholds are also consistent with prior research on explore-exploit strategies of information search (March 1991); consumers may decrease their thresholds when shifting from explore to exploit mode (Huang and Hutchinson 2013) or from search to choice mode (Dellaert and Häubl 2012), whereas inclining thresholds may indicate that initial expectations were too low.Caplin et al. (2011)further demonstrate that reservation values within a satisficing model may systematically vary depending on the size and complexity of the choice set. To empirically test whether the decision threshold decreases within our experimental settings involving large choice sets, in our model, we allow the threshold to change as search progresses. We find across all experiments that shoppers exhibit declining thresholds, with the intercept and slope of the thresholds being related to the duration of search. Allowing the threshold to change over time improves predictions of total fixations, with the degree of threshold decline related to whether shoppers’ fixations are underpredicted/overpredicted when the threshold is fixed at a constant. Importantly, the decision threshold component of our model is meant to be descriptive of observed shopper behavior rather than defining the normative or optimal strategy. As such, we interpret the “exogenous” threshold trend in our model as capturing an explore-exploit strategy that represents strategic planning over a long time horizon (March 1991,Dellaert and Häubl 2012,Huang and Hutchinson 2013). In contrast, a growing body of literature has explored more “endogenous” short-term planning based on learning and forward-looking considerations (Gabaix et al. 2006,Yang et al. 2015,De los Santos and Koulayev 2017). However, we find only weak evidence in our experiments for one-stage-ahead planning, and thus, we do not include this in our main model.1We instead benchmark our model against models with nonheterogeneous thresholds (i.e., do not vary across individuals) and stochastic accumulators more akin to classic sequential sampling models.
article.3. Model Specification: Our model is designed to capture a shopper’s visual search process within a single web page (Experiments 1 and 2) or computer screen display (Experiment 3). As shown inFigure 1, the web page or screen can be divided up intoJAOIs, each containing product information and clickable links. For each individuali=1,…,I, we observe a sequence oft=1,…,Tieye fixations on AOIs that ends with a click. Note thattrefers to ordered, discrete fixation events rather than continuous clock time. We assume that each fixation is determined by a two-stage decision process (seeFigure 2). In stage 1, the shopper selects which AOI to fixate on. Let shopperi’s choice of which AOI to inspect at fixationtbe denoted byFixi[t]∈{AOI1,…,AOIj,…,AOIJ}. In stage 2, the shopper decides whether to continue search with another fixation or end search by clicking on the fixated AOI. Let shopperi’s choice of whether to continue/click after fixationtbe denoted byClicki[t]∈{0,1}. In Experiments 1 and 2, shoppers could click on a link contained within an AOI on the landing page that brought them to a new page of the store’s website. In Experiment 3, participants selected an option by pressing the keyboard while fixating on an AOI (analogous to a click). The likelihood of observing each shopper’s sequence of fixation and click decisions is given by the following equation, which is a function of the parametersθi={βi,γi,τ0i,ρi,δi}(see subsequent discussion), and takes the product of probabilities across the shopper’sTifixations. In Experiments 1 and 2, there was one “trial” (i.e., one landing page) per shopper, whereas there were multiple trials or choices per participant in Experiment 3 (we drop trial-level subscripts for simplicity): Note that the stage 2 click probability is conditional on the stage 1 fixation decision. Across all experiments and trials in our empirical application, a final choice is always made, soClicki[t]=0(i.e., continue) fort=1,…,Ti−1, followed byClicki[Ti]=1(i.e., click) after the last fixationTi. However, our model framework is flexible enough to accommodate scenarios where shoppers do not click at all or click on multiple options, with only minor modifications. In the following subsections, we describe in detail the specification of the factors that contribute to the decisions in each stage. As summarized inFigure 2, the stage 1 fixations depend on the attractiveness of the AOIs and navigation effort, whereas the stage 2 continue/click decisions depend on whether the value of clicking is greater than a decision threshold. 3.1. Similarly, across all experiments in our empirical application, Likert-scale preference ratings for each product are collected from participants. In Experiments 1 and 2, these ratings efficiently capture integration of the many visual attributes of the product AOIs (e.g., color, neckline, sleeve length, etc.), whereas in Experiment 3, they capture overall preference for familiar snack foods. Our multiattribute model also extends this approach by allowing for additional features (e.g., price), and it is more typical of marketing models of discrete choice (e.g.,Roberts and Urban 1988) and conjoint designs (e.g.,Otter et al. 2008a,Yang et al. 2015). Also, note that for the stage 1 decision of thenextfixation eventt+1, we update the attractiveness values (i.e.,xijm[t+1]=xijm′[t]) and precision (i.e.,τijm[t+1]=τijm′[t]). For Experiments 1 and 2, we assume that individuals start fixating at the top of the screen, with initial variables set accordingly. Specifically, for each person’s first fixation decision, we set the values of the distance variable so that the category AOI and the first row of product AOIs are “near” (whereas the rest are “far”). For Experiment 3, we assume that individuals start fixating at the center of the screen, so distance is the same for all product AOIs for the four-choice trials. The distance is set to near (far) for the central (peripheral) snack items in the 9- and 16-choice trials. 3.2. Stage 2: Click vs. Continue Search In stage 2, the individual decides whether to continue search with another fixation or end search by clicking on an option. This is determined by whether the value of clickingUiClick=ViClick[t]+ϵiClick[t]has crossed a decision thresholdUiThreshold=ViThreshold[t]+ϵiThreshold[t]by fixationt. The shopper’s probability of clickingP(Clicki[t]=1|Fixi[t])is conditional on the stage 1 fixation choice and represents the likelihood thatUiClick[t]>UiThreshold[t]; in other words, the current value of clicking and ending search is greater than the decision threshold. Assuming the error terms follow the type 1 extreme value distribution, we model the probability of clicking as a binary logit:2 The value of clickingViClick[t]can be defined in a number of ways. In most real-world visual search settings (and in our main Experiments 1 and 2), individuals nearly always click on the last AOI they fixated on, so we define the value of clicking based on the attractiveness of the currently fixated AOI, which we denote byAOIj′. Note that this value depends only on the AOI’s updated attractiveness; because the AOI is currently fixated, effort is assumed to be zero (i.e., effort is a sunk cost from stage 1). Based on this, the value of clicking can be written as Consistent with this specification, in Experiment 3, participants indicated their choice by fixating on the AOI while pressing a keyboard. In some settings, there may be an explicitly defined visual search period followed by a separate decision period in which individuals make their final choice (e.g.,Thomas et al. 2021). One way to accommodate this would be to use an alternative specification for the value of clicking based on thehighestAOI attractiveness value so far,ViClick[t]=maxj{Aij′[t]}, and further include an additional probability inEquation (1)that models the final AOI choice as a multinomial logit where the utility of clicking on each AOI depends on its updated attractivenessAij′[t]; note that this is not necessary in Experiments 1–3, in which individuals always click on or select the last fixated AOI. Our model is also distinct from relative evidence accumulator models that allow the probability of choice to vary based on an option’s value relative to the mean of the other items (Thomas et al. 2021). Instead, in our model, AOIs “compete for attention” in stage 1 via a logit specification, and only the value of the currently fixated AOI is compared with the decision threshold in stage 2. Finally, the decision threshold is modeled based on two parameters: an interceptρiand a coefficientδion the number of fixations so fart. In other words,δiis the linear rate at which the decision threshold changes with each fixationt: As discussed earlier, in contrast to one-stage-ahead planning, which represents short-term forward looking and is based on expected value calculations, the threshold trend represents a long-term heuristic approximation of explore-exploit strategies. Much of the literature supports a declining threshold because of rising search costs, declining expectations, and/or behavioral shifts (Adam 2001,Brown et al. 2011,Dellaert and Häubl 2012). Thus, we expect a declining threshold (δi<0), which means that the probability of the shopper clicking on a link within an AOI increases as search progresses. However, the model does not restrict individuals from exhibiting an inclining threshold (δi>0), which can be interpreted as initial expectations being too low. 3.3. Estimation We estimated our model using a standard hierarchical Bayes procedure to capture the heterogeneity across shoppers (Gelman et al. 2014) with the programming language R (refer toTable 2for a summary of estimated parameters).3For the first-stage priors, we assumed the parametersθi={βi,γi,τ0i,ρi,δi}followed a multivariate normal distribution with meanμand precisionΩ. For the second-stage priors,μfollowed a conjugate multivariate normal distribution with meanμ0(a vector of zeros) and precisionΩ0(an identity matrix), andΩfollowed a conjugate Wishart distribution with degrees of freedomr(the number of parameters inθiplus three) and inverse scale matrixΛ(whereΛ−1was an identity matrix). We estimated the parameters using a Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler. We ran three MCMC chains from different starting values for 10,000 iterations each, with the first 9,000 as burn-in. We checked for convergence by determining that the Gelman–Rubin convergence statistic was less than 1.2 for all parameters (Gelman and Rubin 1992). We used the posterior samples to calculate a deviance information criterion (DIC), which is a Bayesian version of the Akaike information criterion (AIC). Like the AIC, the DIC includes a penalty for the number of parameters and may increase or decrease when parameters are removed (Gelman et al. 2014).4 3.4. Posterior Predictive Checks Our goal is to accurately model the search process, so it is important to assess how well our model makes in-sample predictions of each shopper’s sequence of fixations and clicks. We use the parameter estimates (i.e., take draws from each shopper’s posterior samples) to simulate theentiresearch sequence from beginning to end. Importantly, this results in simulated search sequences that include all fixations and ending clicks, without using any of the empirically observed information from search (e.g., true number of fixations). This is distinct from approaches used in sequential sampling estimation procedures where for example, observed AOI fixation times are used as inputs to predict search length and final choices (e.g.,Krajbich et al. 2010,Thomas et al. 2021). We then generate posterior predictive checks (e.g.,Gelman et al. 1996,Sinharay and Stern 2003) by comparing the simulated and observed sequences across a variety of characteristics that summarize different parts of the search process (see Online Appendix C for details). Table 3provides a summary of our posterior predictive checks. First, to examine how well our model captures the sequence of eye fixations, for each simulated sequence we calculate the average AOI hit percentage, fixation share, and first-passage times. The AOI hit percentage refers to the percentage of AOIs that were correctly fixated (or not fixated) in the simulations. Higher percentages indicate better model fit. AOI fixation share is the percentage of fixations that each AOI received. AOI first-passage times are the fixationstat which each AOI was first fixated, which allows us to compare sequences that differ in length and is used in eye-tracking research (Holmqvist et al. 2011) and brand recall (Hutchinson et al. 1994).5A model has better in-sample fit if the simulated AOI fixation shares and first-passage times are more positively correlated with the corresponding measures in the observed fixation sequences. The fixation sequence is an important component of search because it indicates a shopper’s consideration set, the order in which items were added to this set, and what information has been learned. Table 3.Summary of Posterior Predictive Checks Table 3.Summary of Posterior Predictive Checks Next, we look at overall search length as measured by the total number of fixations and the total number of unique AOIs seen, which also indicates the individual’s consideration set. For these continuous values, lower root mean squared error (RMSE) relative to shoppers’ observed fixation sequences indicates better model fit. These measures reflect the accuracy of the decision threshold in determining when search should end. Finally, we look at how accurately our model predicts final AOI choice by looking at the percentage of simulations where the correct AOI was clicked on, as well as the attractiveness of the clicked AOI, calculated based onEquation (3). These measures also reflect the accuracy of the decision threshold and how well shoppers were able to find attractive AOIs on the web page. 3.5. Benchmark Models We test our full model against a series of benchmarks, as summarized inTable 4. The no-preference search benchmark setsβ1=0inEquation (3)such that fixation decisions do not depend on preference. The no-effort search benchmark setsγ1,γ2,γ3=0such that fixation decisions do not depend on search effort. Thus, these benchmarks represent relatively random search compared with the full model. The instant learning benchmark setsτ0=0such that preference is learned instantly after one fixation rather than Bayesianly updated. The static threshold benchmark setsδ=0so that the decision threshold is fixed over time. Table 4.Summary of Benchmark Models Table 4.Summary of Benchmark Models We also include a stochastic accumulator benchmark, which has a stopping rule similar to classic sequential sampling models. The stochastic accumulator benchmark assumes that there is a linear stochastic accumulator for each AOI option that evolves over time, depending on the subjective value of the options, until one of the accumulators crosses the decision boundary. Reaction times (i.e., total fixations in our application) are modeled as an inverse Gaussian distribution (Wald 2004,Thomas et al. 2021). Like other sequential sampling models, estimating this model at the individual level requires multiple trials per person because identification relies upon there being a distribution of reaction times. Because we only have one search sequence per shopper in our main Experiments 1 and 2, for these experiments we estimate a population-level version of the stochastic accumulator model using the distribution of total fixationsacrossshoppers and compare this with a population threshold benchmark, in which the decision threshold parametersρandδdo not vary across individuals. For Experiment 3, because there are multiple trials per participant, we can compare our full model, which has heterogeneous threshold parameters, with a stochastic accumulator benchmark with heterogeneous parameters. See Online Appendix D for details.
article.4. Experiment 1: The purpose of Experiment 1 was to collect data for modeling the eye movements of shoppers on the landing page of an online store. We first test whether our model captures the key search characteristics of shopper behavior on the landing page, and then, we examine how the different dynamic components in the model relate to these search characteristics. 4.1. Experiment Design We conducted an incentive-compatible eye-tracking experiment in which female undergraduates at a large university were paid $10 to browse the website of American Apparel’s online store plus were given a 1 in 20 chance of winning a randomly chosen item from their cart up to a maximum price of $75. When shopping for clothing, consumers rely heavily on visual features, so eye tracking was well suited for capturing the accumulation of product information in this setting. Participants in our experiment were informed that their eye movements and mouse activity would be unobtrusively recorded and were calibrated to the eye trackers using the standard procedures recommended for SensoMotoric Instruments eye-tracking systems. Participants were told that they would be shopping for a new top, and they were randomly assigned to either a utilitarian shopping goal (i.e., something to wear for work or class) or a hedonic shopping goal (i.e., something to wear for a party) (see the full instructions in Online Appendix E). The purpose of this manipulation was to induce predictable differences in search and then, test whether these differences could be recovered by our model. We expected that hedonic shoppers would be more stimulus (versus goal) driven compared with utilitarian shoppers and thus, that they would explore the landing page more extensively (Holbrook and Hirschman 1982,Corbetta and Shulman 2002). Participants were then brought to the landing page of American Apparel and given five minutes to shop. We used the actual website’s “New Arrivals” page as the landing page with a few minor adjustments (e.g., making product image sizes uniform, increasing font size, etc.), and we retained all original links to other pages within the store website. As shown inFigure 1(a), we divided up the landing page into 30 product AOIs containing the images and links to specific product choices and 1 category AOI containing all the links to broad product categories. If shoppers clicked on a product AOI, they would be brought to a new web page with detailed information on that product (including the option of adding it their cart). If they instead clicked on a link within the category AOI, they would be brought to a new web page featuring a set of products within a specific category (e.g., “tops,” “dresses,” “skirts,” etc.). All category links and the first row of products were visible upon arrival to the website, whereas the remaining five rows could only be seen by scrolling down the screen. After the first click, shoppers could not return to the landing page, but they were free to visit any other part of the website and edit their cart by adding or removing items. Eye-tracking data and mouse clicks were collected throughout the entire shopping trip. We restricted data collection to a three-week period so that the product selection would not change substantially. After three months, participants were contacted by email to complete a follow-up survey for an additional $10 and a chance to win a $100 American Apparel gift card. They rated how much they liked the 30 landing page products, the products from their carts (which may or may not have been displayed on the landing page), and a random sample of 30–35 products from other participants’ carts. For each product, the image and name were presented, and participants were asked to answer “How much do you like this item?” on a seven-point Likert scale (1 = “don’t like at all,” 7 = “like very much”). Products were presented in random order without prices. These ratings provide an empirical, individual-level estimate of the true preference values, and they allow us to generally assess how well shoppers were able to find products they liked and how stable these preferences were. Collecting product ratings either before or after the main experimental task is common within research employing psychophysical or neurophysiological methods to study preference and choice (Krajbich et al. 2010,Towal et al. 2013). Of the 84 total participants, 10 were excluded from the analyses because of eye-tracker calibration issues, leaving 74 shoppers for the descriptive analysis. Among these participants, 7 did not complete the follow-up survey (i.e., did not have product liking ratings available), leaving 67 shoppers total for the modeling portion of the analyses. 4.2. Descriptive Analysis We focus on search within the landing page, which was common across all shoppers, up until their first click taking them away from the landing page. Among the 74 shoppers with valid eye-tracking data, there were on average 31.85 (standard deviation (SD) = 30.90) total fixations, with 59% of fixations on the product AOIs. Shoppers fixated on 10.58 (SD= 9.50) unique AOIs or about a third of the 31 AOIs displayed. Most shoppers clicked on the category AOI (78%) rather than on one of the product AOIs (22%). Shoppers who clicked on one of the product AOIs made more fixations (M=55.75,SD=41.24) than those who clicked on the category AOI (M=25.26,SD=23.90,t(72)=3.80,p<0.001). The shorter search among category AOI clickers could be because of disliking the initial selection of products presented, wanting to search more efficiently via a specific category link (e.g., the “tops” category), or simply not being interested in a top and wanting to explore a different clothing category altogether. During the remainder of the shopping trip following the first click, on average, individuals visited 5.77 (SD= 3.23) additional web pages, and they found 2.14 (SD= 1.17) items to put in their cart, totaling $93.68 (SD= 61.15) in value. Consistent with our predictions that hedonic shoppers would search more extensively, we found that shoppers in the hedonic goal condition made more product AOI fixations (Mhedonic=34.35,SD=38.13) compared with the utilitarian goal condition (Mutil=18.43,SD=20.92,t(72)=2.27,p=0.026). The difference is more pronounced in the bottom three rows of products (Mdiff=9.15,t(72)=2.92,p=0.004) compared with the top three rows (Mdiff=6.78,t(72)=1.55,p=0.126). This difference in effect size may be because of initial exploration by both groups at the top of the page diluting the effect. Although the manipulation is not the focus of our analyses, the observed effects increase external validity and allow us to examine whether our model is able to capture and potentially explain simple effects that have been reported in the literature on shopping behavior. We also explore whether landing page decisions are predictive of subsequent search by regressing total additional web pages visited and shopping cart value (i.e., dollar amount and total items) on the number of landing page fixations. We find that shoppers who spent more time searching on the landing page later explored less of the rest of the store’s website, with about one less unique web page per 20 additional fixations (β=−0.04,t=−3.33,p=0.003,R2=0.12). They also put $0.42 less in their shopping carts per additional fixation (β=−0.42,t=−1.85,p=0.068,R2=0.03) or about one less item per 100 additional fixations (β=−0.01,t=−2.16,p=0.034,R2=0.03). This suggests that shoppers who leave the landing page quickly are more focused on a specific purchase than those who linger and explore the presented products. More generally, changes to the landing page may impact both search behaviors within the landing page and more downstream shopping behaviors. At the end of this section, we discuss these implications with a series of counterfactual simulations where we manipulate the arrangement of landing page products. 4.3. Parameter Estimation Results For the model estimation, we include the fixation data from the 67 participants with valid eye-tracking data and product liking ratings. The total number of observations for this analysis was 1,969 eye fixations across the 67 shoppers. We report the parameter estimation results inTable 5, which shows the posterior means (μ), the 95% credible intervals (CIs), and the posterior variancesσ2(derived from the diagonal elements of the posterior precision matrixΩ). For each shopper, the MCMC procedure gives us an individual-level distribution of posterior samples. The posterior meansμand variancesσ2refer to the means and variances of these samples across shoppers (i.e., population sample mean and variance). When discussing aggregate model estimation results, we refer to the population sample means. When examining the heterogeneity across shoppers, we refer to the individual-level sample means. Table 5.Experiment 1 Full Model Parameter Estimates Table 5.Experiment 1 Full Model Parameter Estimates 4.3.1. Attractiveness Parameters. The variables included in calculating AOI attractivenessAij[t]inEquation (3)were product preference, price, an intercept for out-of-budget products, and an intercept for the category AOI. Product preference was measured using the seven-point liking ratings (1 = “don’t like at all,” 7 = “like very much”) reported by each shopper. On average, among all the products that shoppers rated in the follow-up survey, only 8% were liked more than the products in their final shopping carts. This suggests that shoppers were pretty good at finding products they liked, and it lends validity to the liking rating as a measure of stable product preferences. For each shopper, we set the true preference values of the product AOIs,xij1*, to her self-reported liking ratings (mean centered to range from −3 to +3) and the prior meanx0i1to be the average of her ratings across all products. Thus, product preferences began at a neutral starting point (i.e., each shopper’s average) and could be updated up or down to approach the true values. We estimated a heterogeneous prior precisionτ0ifor preference, which allows the perceived preferencesxij1[t]to be Bayesianly updated at different rates across individuals, as described byEquations (5)and (6). Similarly, initial beliefs about product prices were set to a neutral midpoint (0), with true prices set to the real website prices (mean centered and divided by $10, ranging from −2.5 to 2.3). The out-of-budget intercept (1 = out of budget, 0 = within budget) captures nonlinearities in price effects for products with prices over the given $75 budget (true prices for out-of-budget products were set to zero). We included an intercept for the category AOI (1 = category AOI, 0 = product AOI) because it did not possess any inherent measurable features. These variables are relatively objective and can be learned by shoppers “at a glance.” Additionally, the out-of-budget and category coefficients are difficult to separately identify from a learning rate. Thus, we set their prior means and precisions to zero, so their perceived values are instantly updated to their true values after one fixation. (See Online Appendix F for robustness checks with different learning rates for price.) As expected,Table 5reports a positive preference coefficientβ1, so individuals were more likely to fixate (and click) on more preferred products. The price and out-of-budget coefficients (β2andβ3) were not significant (i.e., the 95% CI contains zero). Because shoppers were “playing with house money,” it is likely that price did not greatly impact search. These results are also consistent with prior eye-tracking experiments (Chandon et al. 2007) and in-store observational research (Dickson and Sawyer 1990) demonstrating that shoppers often give little or no attention to price. In our shopping setting, holistic product preference is the main determinant of product AOI attractiveness. The category interceptβ4is also positive, consistent with the popularity of clicking on the category AOI in Experiment 1 (78% of shoppers). Category AOI clickers had higher posterior sample means for the category intercept (M=3.92,SD=1.52) than product AOI clickers (M=1.64,SD=2.02,t(65)=4.54,p<0.001). In other words, unsurprisingly, shoppers who found the category AOI to be relatively more attractive tended to click on the category AOI. 4.3.2. Effort Parameters. The variables included in calculating AOI fixation effortEij[t]inEquation (4)were scroll, distance, and stickiness. The scroll variable indicated whether the shopper needed to scroll down from the initially visible window to see an AOI. The distance variable indicated that an AOI was positioned nonadjacent to the previously fixated AOI, thus requiring far (versus near) eye movements, and it captured the effort required to move the eye to AOIs that were in the more central versus more peripheral areas of the shopper’s field of view. We used contrast coding (−1 and +1) for scroll and distance to reduce correlation with other variables.Table 5reports negative coefficients for both scroll (γ1) and distance (γ2), so shoppers are less likely to fixate on an AOI if they need to put in the visuomotor effort to scroll down the screen or if the AOI is farther away from their last fixation. Shoppers with more negative scroll coefficients (i.e., less likely to scroll down) also made fewer fixations (r=0.74,t(65)=8.76,p<0.001). Interestingly, shoppers with more negative distance coefficients (i.e., preferred closer AOIs) actually mademoretotal fixations (r=−0.47,t(65)=4.25,p<0.001). One interpretation is that shoppers who search longer examine product AOIs more systematically (e.g., row by row) rather than jumping around the screen. Stickiness referred to the number of fixations since an AOI was first fixated on (set to zero for nonfixated AOIs). The stickiness coefficientγ3is negative, which allows shoppers to explore new AOIs and counterbalances the preference to fixate (or refixate) on nearby AOIs (as captured by distance) and more liked AOIs (as captured by preference). This is particularly important in modeling large choice sets, and it prevents simulations from getting “stuck” on early highly preferred AOIs. 4.3.3. Decision Parameters. Finally, the decision parameters are those that determine the rate at which shoppers update their product liking preferences (τ0) as well as the stopping rule via the decision threshold (interceptρand slopeδ). Note that we estimatelog(τ0)to constrainτ0>0. In our assumed Bayesian updating process,τ0represents thepriorprecision for preference, so lower values make it easier to update away from the prior mean and therefore, correspond to faster learning (or equivalently, relatively stronger information signals). Conversely, higher values make it harder to update away from the prior mean and therefore, correspond to slower learning.τ0determines both the rate and curvature of the learning curve. For example, whenlog(τ0)=−3, 95% of the true preference value of a product AOI is learned after one fixation (i.e., close to “instant learning”). Whenlog(τ0)=−1, 73% of the true value is learned on the first fixation, and 93% is learned by the fifth fixation. Forlog(τ0)= 2, these values are 12% and 40%, and forlog(τ0)= 4, these values are 2% and 9%. Less obvious, but equally important, is the fact that the amount of curvature decreases asτ0increases, going from a step function forlog(τ0)= −3 to moderate curvature forlog(τ0)= 2 to being essentially linear forlog(τ0)≥4. Curvature allows AOIs with higher true preference values to “catch up” to AOIs with lower values but more fixations early in the search process. For example, whenlog(τ0)<0, it takes only one fixation for an AOI to catch up to another AOI that has half its value but five fixations; whenlog(τ0)=2, it takes two fixations, and whenlog(τ0)≥4, it takes three fixations. As shown inTable 5,log(τ0)has a relatively high posterior mean of 4.53 (and a 95% CI of [3.04, 6.32]), indicating that true preference is learned relatively slowly; however, the learning curve is linear, so AOIs that are “behind” would take longer to catch up. Turning to the heterogeneity in learning rates, we find that shoppers who clicked on the category AOI were relatively slow learners with significantly higherlog(τ0)(M=4.66,SD=0.47) compared with those who clicked on a product AOI (M=4.09,SD=1.59,t(65)=2.30,p=0.025). One possibility is that because of their slower learning rates, for category AOI clickers, the category AOI attractiveness reached the stage 2 decision threshold before they fully learned their true preferences for the product AOIs, or it may be that shoppers opt for the category AOI when they cannot decide between two options; to account for this would require a slight modification to our model that allows the value of the category AOI to evolve with potential indecision or relative values. We also find that among product AOI clickers, there was a positive correlation betweenlog(τ0)and total fixations (r=0.52,t(11)=2.04,p=0.066), which is consistent with the logic that slower learners need more fixations to learn their true preferences and thus, search longer. These results illustrate the importance of allowing for heterogeneity in how quickly shoppers learn information. The two parameters that define the decision threshold are the interceptρand the slopeδ. The posterior means of these parameters allow the threshold to start high (ρ=8.39) and decline with each passing fixation (δ=−0.13). Intuitively, the high intercept encourages exploration because only an extremely attractive AOI will be clicked. As search progresses, the negative slope encourages the shopper to make a click decision. Allowing the threshold parameters to vary across individuals helps to capture the heterogeneity in search length or total fixations. Shoppers who made more fixations had thresholds that started at a higher interceptρ(r=0.75,t(65)=9.03,p<0.001) and decreased more slowly, as indicated by the slopeδ(r=0.83,t(65)=11.85,p<0.001). In summary, we find that our model of fixation and click decisions generates intuitive results regarding the trade-off between AOI attractiveness and fixation effort. Specifically, shoppers tend to fixate on AOIs that contain more attractive products, are visually closer, and do not require initial scrolling down the screen. Bayesian updating allows us to determine how fast different shoppers learn about their preferences for the AOIs. We find that shoppers exhibit declining decisions thresholds, consistent with prior research on explore-exploit heuristics. 4.4. Model Performance 4.4.1. Posterior Predictive Checks and Benchmark Comparisons. To assess how well our model captures individual shopper search on the landing page, we use each shopper’s posterior samples to simulate 1,000 fixation sequences. We then generate posterior predictive checks by comparing the simulated and observed sequences across a variety of characteristics that summarize the different stages of search. As shown inTable 6, we compare the full model with a series of benchmarks based on the DIC values and the posterior predictive checks. The full model has the lowest DIC, indicating the best in-sample fit. We first focus on comparing the (1) full model with the benchmarks with heterogeneous parameters (2)–(5). In the next subsection on heterogeneity, we then compare benchmarks (6) and (7), which have population-level or nonheterogeneous decision threshold parameters. Table 6.Experiment 1 Comparison of Full Model (1) with Benchmark Models (2)–(7) Table 6.Experiment 1 Comparison of Full Model (1) with Benchmark Models (2)–(7) Note. Asterisks indicate posterior predictive checks where the benchmark performs significantly worse relative to the Full model based on appropriate pairedt-tests of the values across shoppers. *p< 0.05; **p< 0.01; ***p< 0.001. To compare the models on how well they capture the eye fixation sequences, we look at AOI hit percentage, fixation share, and first-passage times. For the full model, across the 67 shoppers in our sample, the average AOI hit percentage was 78%, the average correlation between the observed and simulated AOI fixation shares was 0.54, and the average correlation between the observed and simulated AOI first-passage times was 0.40. We would expect models that assume that fixations are more randomly determined to perform worse. Indeed,Table 6shows that the no-preference and no-effort benchmarks (in which we remove preference and effort, respectively, from the fixation decisions) are worst at predicting the fixation sequences. The instant learning benchmark also performs poorly. As mentioned previously, the learning rate of product AOI preferences determines both how quickly true values are learned and how quickly AOIs catch up to each other in terms of attractiveness, which corresponds to the order in which AOIs are inspected. The static threshold benchmark mainly changes the decision threshold stopping rule, and thus, it performs just slightly worse, likely related to worse performance in predicting search length, as we discuss next. We can assess how well the models predict search length by looking at total fixations and unique AOIs seen. Our model simulates an average of 28.81 total fixations across shoppers, which is very close to the observed average of 29.39 fixations (among the 67 shoppers with valid eye-tracking and preference rating data), with a low RMSE of 5.99. Similarly, our model simulates 9.61 total unique AOIs, which is close to the observed average of 10.06, with an RMSE of 2.75. Again, the no-preference and no-effort benchmarks perform poorly, indicating that the sequence of fixation decisions is closely tied to search length. We also expect that changing the decision threshold specification, which determines the search stopping rule, should have a big impact on search length. Aligned with this, the static threshold benchmark also performs poorly, particularly in terms of total fixations, providing further evidence in support of a decreasing threshold. We can compare model performance in terms of predicting the final AOI choice by looking at the correct AOI click percentage and clicked AOI attractiveness. If we treat the click as a random draw with equal probabilities across all 31 AOIs, then the average percentage of correct simulated clicks (61%) is well above chance (3%).6The mean simulated attractiveness of the clicked AOI is 3.59, which is close to the observed mean of 3.75, with RMSE of 1.02. The no-effort benchmark performs the worst, suggesting that accounting for the physical effort of making eye fixations or the arrangement of the items on the screen is important for predicting final choice. 4.4.2. Heterogeneity. We also investigate how well our model captures heterogeneity across shoppers.Figure 3plots the observed versus simulated total fixations (Figure 3, (a)–(d)) and clicked AOI attractiveness (Figure 3, (e)–(h)) for the full model and the three benchmarks, with each point representing one shopper. The static threshold and stochastic accumulator benchmarks have different stopping rule specifications, whereas the population threshold benchmark has nonheterogeneous threshold parameters for better comparison with the stochastic accumulator benchmark. The full model (Figure 3, (a) and (e)) captures the heterogeneity across shoppers the best, with the overlaying regression line and individual points falling close to the identity line for both total fixations, withβ=1.17(t(65)=39.91,p<0.001,R2=0.96), and clicked AOI attractiveness, withβ=0.99(t(65)=11.82,p<0.001,R2=0.68). The static threshold benchmark results in shoppers falling farther from the identity line compared with the full model for total fixations (seeFigure 3, (b) and (f)). The regression line is, however, close to the identity line withβ=1.03(t(65)=12.66,p<0.001,R2=0.70), indicating good performance on average but relatively poor performance in representing heterogeneity (i.e., lowerR2: 0.70 versus 0.96). When the threshold trendδis removed, total fixations for shoppers with thresholds that decline at a faster rate (based on the full model) are overpredicted (i.e., search too long), whereas shoppers whose thresholds decline at a slower rate are underpredicted (i.e., search ends too soon) because the threshold interceptρis “misestimated” up or down to compensate for the lack of slope. Consistent with this, the correlation between the individual-level posterior means ofδfrom the full model and the difference or “error” between total fixations predicted by the static threshold benchmark and observed total fixations is negative and significant (r=−0.69,t(65)=−7.74,p<0.001). The static threshold performs similarly to the full model in terms of clicked AOI attractiveness, withβ=1.09(t(65)=8.91,p<0.001,R2=0.54). InTable 6, we see that the stochastic accumulator and population threshold benchmarks, which have nonheterogeneous stopping rule parameters, perform particularly poorly in terms of predicting search length. As shown inFigure 3, (c) and (g), the stochastic accumulator benchmark simulates roughly the same total fixations across all shoppers, withβ=11.49(t(65)=5.79,p=0.051,R2=0.04), but it is able to capture some of the heterogeneity in clicked AOI attractiveness, withβ=0.80(t(65)=6.68,p<0.001,R2=0.40). In panels (d) and (h) inFigure 3, we see that the population threshold benchmark performs similarly to the static threshold model, withβ=1.17(t(65)=13.08,p<0.001,R2=0.72), with the threshold slopeδfrom the full model again being correlated with the error in simulated total fixations (r=−0.85,t(65)=−12.92,p<0.001). Note that although the estimated threshold does not vary across shoppers, the population threshold benchmark is still able to capture some of the heterogeneity in total fixations because of the heterogeneity built into other parameters, notably the coefficientsβ1on product preference andβ2on the category AOI indicator. Moreover, the model does well in capturing the heterogeneity in clicked AOI attractiveness (β=1.03,t(65)=10.57,p<0.001,R2=0.63). Overall, both the heterogeneous and (more comparable) nonheterogeneous versions of our decision threshold stopping rule outperform a model with a stopping rule akin to sequential sampling models (i.e., the stochastic accumulator benchmark). Finally, consistent with the observed findings, our model simulations predicted more fixations on product AOIs for hedonic shoppers (Mhedonic=31.94,SD=22.89) compared with utilitarian shoppers (Mutil=26.11,SD=18.55,t(65)=1.15,p=0.254), although this difference was not statistically significant. We used the same model for all shoppers, so these consistent differences are generated by the heterogeneity in the parameter estimates built into the hierarchical Bayes procedure, and they suggest that our model can be generalized across shoppers with different goals. 4.4.3. Summary of Full vs. Benchmark Model Comparisons. In summary, our two-stage model of fixation and click decisions outperforms various benchmarks with specific model components removed (i.e., the no-preference, no-effort, instant learning, and static threshold benchmarks). These results provide evidence that subjective product value and visuomotor effort are important determinants of the search process, that information accumulation is incremental rather than instant, and that the decision threshold decreases over time. The different benchmarks tend to perform worse for multiple measures of the search process, highlighting interdependencies between the different stages of search within a single web page or screen display. Certain benchmarks perform particularly poorly for specific measures; for example, the no-effort benchmark is worst at predicting fixation sequences, whereas the static threshold benchmark greatly overestimates search length, suggesting that search components, such as effort and the threshold slope, are more important for these processes. Finally, the full model is able to capture heterogeneity in search length and quality across shoppers, even with a single fixation sequence per shopper. Accurately simulating differences in search length across shoppers is worse without a dynamic decision threshold (i.e., the static threshold benchmark), is worse without heterogeneity in the decision threshold (i.e., the population threshold benchmark), and is not possible with a stochastic accumulator stopping rule. 4.5. Counterfactual Analysis: Preference-Based Product Layouts Online retailers often provide shoppers with search refinement tools, such as the option to organize products on a page by price or popularity. Retailers may use shoppers’ past browsing behavior to make recommendations, customize web-page layouts, or predict click-through rates (Hauser et al. 2009,De los Santos and Koulayev 2017).Häubl and Trifts (2000)demonstrate that giving consumers recommendations based on self-reported preferences leads to smaller consideration sets but better purchase decisions, whereasDiehl and Zauberman (2005)find that improving (versus declining) orderings results in overall higher choice evaluations. Thus, there is evidence suggesting that either a “best-first” (Häubl and Trifts 2000) or “worst-first” (Diehl and Zauberman 2005) product layout might improve choices depending on the psychological mechanisms at work in the decision process. Similarly, we are interested in how search within a web page would change with different product layouts. Thus, we conducted a counterfactual analysis in which we used the parameter samples from our model to simulate fixation sequences on the landing page with alternative worst-first and best-first product layouts (i.e., products ordered from worst to best and best to worst, respectively, going from left to right and from top to bottom on the page) based on each shopper’s self-reported liking ratings of the products. In Experiment 2, we explicitly manipulated product layout to empirically test the predictions made by this counterfactual analysis. The results of the simulated fixation sequences predict that shoppers would make more fixations in the worst-first condition (M=29.35,SD=21.27) compared with the best-first condition (M=28.28,SD=20.45;paired-t(66)=2.04,p=0.045), with the difference across individuals ranging from −5 to +25 fixations (worst first minus best first). Worst-first condition shoppers were also predicted to fixate on more unique AOIs (M=10.10,SD=7.92) compared with shoppers in the best-first condition (M=9.22,SD=7.39,paired-t(66)=2.96,p=0.004), with the difference ranging from −1 to +14. However, the simulations also predicted that shoppers would click on less preferred AOIs (i.e., lower liking ratings) in the worst-first condition (M=4.96,SD=2.11) compared with the best-first condition (M=5.78,SD=1.54;paired-t(66)=4.89,p<0.001).7These differences also hold if we order the products based on population-average preference ratings rather than individual-level ratings (see Online Appendix F for details). These results suggest that changing the product layout on a single web page based on shopper preferences can have a significant impact on search length, as measured by total fixations and total unique AOIs, as well as on how well shoppers are able to find an attractive link to click on. Our finding that the best-first layout results in better choice quality is consistent withHäubl and Trifts (2000). We are also able to quantify the potential harm of a worst-first layout, which leads to longer search and thus, a larger consideration set but ultimately, leads to lower choice quality as well as potentially fewer pages visited downstream, fewer items in the shopping cart, and overall lower customer satisfaction.
article.5. Experiment 2: Empirical Test of Count: The main purpose of Experiment 2 was to empirically test the predictions of our counterfactual simulations from Experiment 1 with worst-first and best-first product layouts. We also replicate our main model results from Experiment 1 in a different retail setting with a new sample. 5.1. Experiment Design We conducted a second eye-tracking experiment in which a different set of female undergraduates at the same large university as in Experiment 1 were paid $10 to browse the online store of the clothing retailer Forever 21. All participants were required to complete a prescreening survey at least five to seven days prior to the eye-tracking task. In the survey, participants viewed images of 60 products available for online purchase from Forever 21. They first rated their liking of each product on a seven-point Likert scale, and then, they ranked the products within each point rating to break ties. This gave us a full ranking of all 60 products from each individual. This presurvey ensured that we had corresponding preference ratings for all eye-tracking data, and it allowed us to construct personalized landing pages for the layout conditions, as described. Among the 151 participants, 19 were excluded because of eye-tracker calibration issues, leaving 132 participants total for analysis. During the eye-tracking experiment, participants were brought to a modified version of the Forever 21 landing page and had five minutes to shop, again with a 1 in 20 chance that they might win one of the items in their cart up to $50 plus the difference between the item price and the $50 budget in cash (lower budget than Experiment 1 because Forever 21 had lower prices). All products on the landing page were in budget. The Forever 21 landing page consisted of a category AOI and a grid of 40 product AOIs (seeFigure 1(b)), with the products being a subset of the 60 presented in the prescreening survey. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three landing page layout conditions: (1) random layout (N=41), (2) worst-first layout (N=45), and (3) best-first layout (N=46). In the worst-first and best-first layout conditions, each participant was presented with a personalized landing page in which the products were arranged based on their rankings from the prescreening survey. Participants were not informed of the experimental conditions, and they were again free to shop anywhere on the website once they left the landing page. 5.2. Analysis and Results One objective of Experiment 2 was to test the robustness of the results from Experiment 1 using a different retailer. In the following paragraphs, we describe parallel analyses that were conducted for both experiments. Our model estimation results from Experiment 1 are largely replicated in Experiment 2, with some key differences attributable to differences in shopper preferences and web-page design features. We first look at overall descriptive statistics followed by parameter estimation results, posterior predictive checks, and benchmark model comparisons. Table 7compares descriptive statistics of shopper behavior between Experiments 1 and 2. Across all three Experiment 2 conditions, shoppers made more total fixations and looked at more AOIs compared with Experiment 1. Collapsing across conditions, shoppers made on average 94.03 (SD= 67.66) fixations on the landing page, totaling 12,412 fixation observations. Most shoppers clicked on one of the product AOIs (92% versus 22% in Experiment 1) rather than on the category AOI. Shoppers in Experiment 2 rated higher preference for products (M=3.77,SD=0.75) compared with Experiment 1 (M=3.40,SD=0.80;t(197)=3.17,p=0.002). They also added more products to their carts (M=2.81,SD=1.87), although the total value was lower (M=$56.10,SD=$46.30) because the products were overall lower in price. These differences indicate that the Forever 21 products were more popular among participants compared with the American Apparel products. Applying our model to data involving two different retailers allows us to examine the robustness of our model and general findings of shopper search patterns as well as our model’s ability to provide insights into the possible causes of major differences in behavior. Consistent with Experiment 1, in Experiment 2 we again find that shoppers with more negative scroll coefficientsγ1made fewer fixations (r=0.46,t(130)=5.83,p<0.001), whereas shoppers with more negative distance coefficientsγ2made more fixations (r=−0.39,t(130)=4.76,p<0.001). Again, the rate of the threshold declineδestimated in the full model is related to the overprediction/underprediction of total fixations by the static threshold benchmark; specifically, shoppers with faster declines are overpredicted, whereas shoppers with slower declines are underpredicted (r=−0.73,t(130)=12.26,p<0.001). Like in Experiment 1, the stochastic accumulator benchmark simulates the same total fixations across shoppers, withβ=0.99(t(130)=0.18,p=0.305,R2=0.00), but it is able to capture variation in clicked AOI attractiveness (β=0.91,t(130)=10.52,p<0.001,R2=0.46). This is because of the stopping rule parameters being nonheterogeneous (we only have one reaction time per shopper) but the other parameters that determine fixation probability being heterogeneous. Although the population threshold benchmark underpredicted total fixations in Experiment 1, it overpredicted total fixations in Experiment 2, likely because of shoppers with high total fixations being overweighted and driving the estimation of the decision threshold parameters upward. Despite having a nonheterogeneous threshold, this benchmark is still able to capture some of the heterogeneity across shoppers in terms of total fixations, withβ=0.65(t(130)=7.93,p<0.001,R2=0.32), and clicked AOI attractiveness, withβ=1.16(t(130)=14.54,p<0.001,R2=0.61). 5.3. Summary of Model Estimation Results in Experiments 1 and 2 The key model estimation results from Experiment 1 are largely replicated in Experiment 2. Across both experiments, we find consistent positive coefficients for the attractiveness variables (i.e., preference and the category intercept) and negative coefficients for the effort variables (scroll, distance, and stickiness), which factor into the fixation decisions. Bayesian updating determines a heterogeneous learning rate of product preferences, with slower learners searching longer. Shoppers primarily exhibit declining thresholds, and allowing for a dynamic threshold improves predictions of total fixations (i.e., search duration). Most of the differences between the two experiments stem from participants’ greater preference for the Forever 21 products, which can explain the greater number of fixations, the more clicks on product AOIs, the faster learning rate of product preferences, and the slower threshold decline. The full model is able to capture the heterogeneity across shoppers in terms of predicting total fixations and attractiveness of the clicked AOI, outperforming benchmarks with different model components removed (e.g., preference, effort, threshold dynamics) and different stopping rule specifications. 5.4. Effects of Product Layout In addition to testing the robustness of our model findings, Experiment 2 verifies our counterfactual predictions regarding product layouts from Experiment 1. We first replicated the counterfactual simulations using parameters obtained from re-estimating the model on data from only the 41 random layout condition participants in Experiment 2. We then discuss the results obtained via direct experimental manipulation by comparing the best-first and worst-first layout conditions. 5.4.1. Counterfactual Simulation Predictions. The counterfactual simulations for Experiment 2 were done using parameters (i.e., posterior samples) obtained from estimating our model on the 41 random condition participants and constructing hypothetical best-first and worst-first product layouts using these participants’ self-reported liking ratings of the 40 landing page products. As in Experiment 1, the worst-first layout is predicted to result in more total fixations (Mworst-first=103.34,SD=70.36) compared with the best-first layout (Mbest-first=83.35,SD=65.87; paired-t(40)=7.28,p<0.001), with differences ranging from −17 to +70 fixations. The worst-first layout is also predicted to result in fixations on more unique AOIs (Mworst-first=24.60,SD=10.99) compared with the best-first layout (Mbest-first=19.77,SD=10.94; paired-t(40)=6.60,p<0.001). Finally, we predict the worst-first layout to result in shoppers clicking on less preferred AOIs (Mworst-first=1.04,SD=1.40) compared with the best-first layout (Mbest-first=2.04,SD=0.91; paired-t(40)=6.22,p<0.001). 5.4.2. Experimental Manipulation Results. Next, we examine the results of the experimental manipulation of product layouts. Consistent with the counterfactual predictions of both experiments, shoppers in the worst-first condition made more fixations (Mworst-first=109.38,SD=59.83) compared with the best-first condition (Mbest-first=78.61,SD=57.03;t(89)=2.51,p=0.014). In the worst-first condition, shoppers also fixated on more unique AOIs (Mworst-first=28.31,SD=11.63) compared with the best-first condition (Mbest-first=20.13,SD=14.21;t(89)=3.00,p=0.003). Also consistent with the simulation predictions, worst-first shoppers clicked on less preferred AOIs (Mworst-first=0.63,SD=1.85) compared with best-first shoppers (Mbest-first=2.34,SD=1.28;t(89)=5.14,p<0.001). In summary, the counterfactual simulation predictions from Experiment 1 were replicated using data from the random layout condition in Experiment 2, which consists of an entirely new sample of participants and products. The Experiment 2 counterfactuals also predicted larger effects of the layout manipulations compared with Experiment 1. We believe that the greater number and appeal of the Forever 21 products enhanced the layout effects in Experiment 2. Critically, the counterfactual predictions are empirically validated by the results from the experimentally manipulated worst-first and best-first conditions in Experiment 2. These robust and consistent results are a strong test of the validity of our model in simulating accurate counterfactuals, at least regarding product layouts. They also illustrate the trade-off between designing product orderings to increase browsing behavior versus to help consumers find their most preferred products quickly.
