article.7. General Discussion: Every retailing website starts with a landing page that provides a common starting point for shoppers and can be customized by retailers in different ways to influence the overall shopping experience and downstream behaviors. The main goal of our paper was to construct a flexible, psychologically grounded model that accurately represents the complex, split-second search process of shoppers within a single web page, combining elements of sequential sampling models from psychology and sequential search models from economics. Across the three empirical applications of our model, we consistently find evidence that eye fixation decisions depend on AOI attractiveness and navigation effort, that people update the subjective value of AOI options gradually rather than instantly, and that the decision threshold decreases with each passing fixation. Our model can capture and simulate each shopper’s sequence of eye fixations and first clicks on the landing page from beginning to end. The estimation results and benchmark comparisons demonstrate the importance of accounting for fixation effort, exploration, and systematic search, highlighting the complexity of search patterns within even a single web page. Importantly, we used a combination of counterfactual simulations and experimental manipulation (i.e., shopper-specific customized landing pages) to test the effects of preference-based product layouts. Our results suggest that although arranging products in worst-first order can increase time spent on the page and the size of the consideration set (which may be desirable for firms in the short run), arranging products in descending or best-first order can help shoppers find better options faster (which may increase customer satisfaction and be desirable in the long run). Although the worst-first layout represents one extreme, in many cases, firms may want to strategically place less-preferred items near the top (e.g., clearance items, sponsored products, ads, etc.). The flexibility of our modeling approach potentially provides firms with a tool to simulate or forecast shopper response to different web-page layouts. Additionally, although our main analyses utilized individual-level preference data, we demonstrate that our model is capable of within-subject and out-of-sample predictions as well as alternative layout simulations using population-average preference ratings. Thus, we show that our results are generalizable to a variety of settings and that individual-level data and analyses are not absolutely necessary to generate meaningful insights. The landing page (or any other web page) may even be optimized depending on the managerial goals. To illustrate, using data from Experiment 2, we simulated shopper search with a “hybrid” landing page layout with the product AOIs arranged from best to worst, except with the last row of “worst” products shuffled to the top. Compared with the best-first layout, this hybrid layout resulted in a larger consideration set with more unique AOIs seen (Mhybrid=20.54,SD=11.26;Mbest-first=19.73,SD=10.99; paired-t(40)=2.49,p=0.017), but it maintained the same search quality in terms of clicked AOI attractiveness (Mhybrid=1.96,SD=1.26;Mbest-first=2.06,SD=0.88; paired-t(40)=0.99,p=0.329). This hybrid layout represents one design that encourages shoppers to view more products while still finding preferred items. Our analyses also uncovered interesting systematic differences across experiments. For example, the learning rate for product preference was faster in Experiments 2 and 3, which involved products that were more familiar to shoppers compared with Experiment 1. Consistent with prior work (Caplin et al. 2011,Reutskaja et al. 2011), the estimated search parameters also differed with different choice-set sizes. Interestingly, shoppers in Experiment 1 mostly made their first click on a category AOI, whereas those in Experiment 2 tended to click on one of the product AOIs. There are several interpretations for this behavior; for example, shoppers rated the Experiment 1 landing page products (from American Apparel) lower in terms of liking compared with the Experiment 2 products (from Forever 21), so perhaps this lower preference led them to explore the category links instead. Overall, it is important for firms to consider how differences in choice-set size as well as consumer preference and familiarity with products may impact search across retail settings. For example, recent eye-tracking work has explored how brand utility and familiarity influence search and choice behavior (Martinovici et al. 2023,Ursu et al. 2024). Although our model was constructed to capture search among category AOIs and product AOIs, there is room to extend our model to a wider range of web-page layouts as well as to different stages of the full shopping trip. In our analyses, we treated the category AOI as an outside option for when the product AOIs do not meet the threshold. However, consumers may select the category AOI for other reasons as well: for example, if there is indecision among the competing product AOIs or if they have other shopping goals in mind and are interested in other product categories. Our model could also be extended to account for the value of AOIs to evolve depending on the relative value of nearby AOIs (e.g., contrast effects), the potential to find better items with more search (i.e., short-term forward looking), or even switching from search to choice mode. Additionally, with the increased use of product sorting and recommendation algorithms, including personalized product orderings, shoppers may adjust their behavior depending on the sorting tools used. We focused on modeling the landing page in Experiments 1 and 2 to be able to directly compare shoppers, but the search process can be generalized and applied to other web pages as well. Finally, in our experiments, we incorporated several forms of exogenous variation to test how well our model accounts for differences in search behavior. In Experiment 1, we manipulated participants’ shopping goals, which resulted in marginally significant differences in search length, and directionally consistent model predictions. In Experiment 2, we manipulated the product layouts using participants’ preference ratings. In Experiment 3, subjects were exposed to different choice-set sizes. An interesting area for future research would be to investigate other ways to manipulate (and use models to predict) shopper goals and subsequent search behavior, perhaps with stronger conditions that may overcome the potential dominating effects of incentive-compatible designs (i.e., the intrinsic goal of maximizing one’s reward from the experiment). In conclusion, our two-stage model of information search within a web page reveals key insights into how individuals visually navigate realistic and complex shopping environments that contain large sets of options and require weighing time or effort costs against the benefits of search. In particular, our model strikes a balance between accounting for known psychological processes, features of the virtual environment, and assumptions about individuals as utility-maximizing agents. We demonstrate that our model is applicable to both more realistic and more stylized laboratory settings. We hope that future work introduces additional models that can capture consumer attention and decision processes within increasingly complex and dynamic virtual shopping environments. These types of models can take advantage of bridging the gap between the descriptive versus normative approaches of psychological and economic models of information and product search. Follow-up work may also determine the degree to which consumers are able to exhibit optimal (or perhaps “near-optimal”) search behavior: for example, by employing sensitivity analyses to assess how well shoppers search compared with if they had alternative search parameter values.
